deepseek-r1-distill-llama-8b.text: Okay, so I'm trying to figure out the diagnosis for this 35-year-old man with a daily headache.
Let me go through the information step by step.
He has a headache that occurs twice a day, every day for the past three weeks.
Each attack lasts about an hour and wakes him up from sleep.
That makes me think it's something that happens during sleep, maybe a sleep disorder.
The headache is described as deep and excruciating, which is pretty intense.
It's also limited to the right side of his head, so it's not a generalized headache like a migraine or tension headache.
He also mentions tearing and reddening of the right eye along with nasal stuffiness.
Tearing and redness in one eye could indicate some kind of irritation or inflammation.
Nasal stuffiness suggests possible sinus issues.
Maybe it's related to sinus headaches, but I'm not sure yet.
The neurological examination is normal, so there's no evidence of brain issues like strokes or tumors.
That makes me think it's not something like a migraine with aura, which can sometimes have neurological symptoms, but since the exam is normal, maybe it's more of a functional issue.
Putting this together, the fact that the headache occurs upon waking and is on one side makes me think of cluster headaches.
Cluster headaches are known to occur more frequently, often waking the person from sleep, and they can be very intense.
They typically affect one side of the head and can be accompanied by tearing and redness in the eye, which aligns with the patient's symptoms.
The nasal stuffiness might be due to sinus involvement, which is common in cluster headaches.
I should also consider other possibilities, like sinus headaches or migraines, but cluster headaches fit better here because of the frequency, the timing, and the one-sided symptoms.
Since the neurological exam is normal, it's less likely to be a neurological disorder like a brain tumor or stroke.
So, the most likely diagnosis is cluster headache.

deepseek-r1-distill-qwen-14b.text: Okay, so I'm trying to figure out the diagnosis for this 35-year-old man with daily headaches.
Let's break down the information given.
He's been having two attacks a day for the past three weeks.
Each attack lasts about an hour and wakes him up from sleep.
That's interesting because it's happening at night, which might point towards certain types of headaches that are worse in the morning or wake you up.
The pain is described as deep and excruciating, localized to the right side of his head.
He also mentions tearing and reddening of the right eye along with nasal stuffiness.
Those symptoms—tearing, redness, and congestion—on one side make me think of something related to the trigeminal nerve or possibly sinus issues.
He doesn't have any neurological deficits, so it's probably not a stroke or something like that.
The fact that it's bilateral in terms of attacks but unilateral in terms of symptoms (right side) makes me think of something that affects one side but occurs multiple times.
I remember that cluster headaches are characterized by severe, unilateral headaches that often wake the person up.
They're usually around the eye and come with symptoms like tearing, redness, and stuffiness.
They can occur multiple times a day, which fits the two attacks per day.
Another possibility could be migraines, but migraines typically have other symptoms like nausea, sensitivity to light, and they're not usually associated with such clear unilateral eye symptoms.
Plus, migraines can occur on either side, but this seems more consistent with cluster headaches.
Trigeminal neuralgia is another option, but that's more about sharp, shooting pains and is usually not associated with the eye symptoms described here.
It's also less likely to occur in clusters of attacks.
So putting it all together: severe, unilateral headaches with autonomic symptoms (tearing, red eye, stuffiness) that wake him up at night, occurring multiple times a day.
That points strongly towards cluster headaches.

qwq-32b.text: The patient is a 35-year-old man with daily headaches for three weeks.
He has two attacks a day, each lasting about an hour, and they wake him from sleep.
The headaches are severe, unilateral (right side), and associated with tearing and reddening of the right eye, plus nasal stuffiness.
Hmm, the key points here are the unilateral headache, the associated autonomic symptoms like tearing (lacrimation), eye redness (conjunctival injection), and nasal congestion.
The timing—waking him from sleep and occurring twice daily for three weeks.
Cluster headaches are classic for this presentation.
They typically occur in cyclical patterns, hence "cluster" periods, which can last weeks.
The side is usually consistent, so right side here.
The symptoms on the same side as the headache (like ptosis or miosis might also occur but not mentioned here) are part of the cranial autonomic features.
The duration of an hour and the frequency (two times a day) fit cluster headache's typical attacks, which can happen up to eight times a day.
The fact that the neurological exam is normal is also important because it rules out other causes like tumors or strokes.
Migraines can have autonomic features but usually last longer and aren't as strictly unilateral with the same timing.
Tension headaches don't have the autonomic symptoms.
Maybe trigeminal neuralgia, but that's more like sharp, electric shocks and not持续 an hour.
So cluster headache is the most likely.
